one_line_takeaway: Contrary to the field's prevailing assumption, DeepJSCC-based semantic communication is empirically and theoretically shown to require 14-16x more white-box channel-injection attack power than classical LDPC-coded SSCC systems to reach the same target distortion, with the gap traced to implicit decoder smoothness from noisy-channel training and to previously unexploited structural weaknesses (Tanner-graph vulnerable sets) in LDPC codes under intelligent attack.
key_finding: At 12dB SNR on the image task, the strongest classical attack (vulnerable set, QPSK, 5/6-LDPC) needs rho*_sscc,1 ~= 7.5e-3 while the strongest semantic attack (PGA) needs rho*_sem,1 ~= 0.11 -- semantic communication requires about 14x more attack power for the same 15dB-PSNR target distortion. On the CSI task at 10dB SNR, rho*_sscc,1 ~= 3.1e-2 (vulnerable set) vs. rho*_sem,1 ~= 0.5, i.e. more than 16x more attack power for the same -16dB NMSE target. Within classical systems, the paper's own vulnerable-set attack needs ~3x less power than the RL/GMS baseline on images and up to 2x less on CSI, showing that exploiting LDPC Tanner-graph structure (short cycles / weak constraint subspaces) is a substantially more efficient attack strategy than a generic learned (DQN) attacker. Within semantic systems, PGA consistently outperforms (needs less power than) the C&W-style baseline across the SNR range. Ablations confirm that targeting the precomputed 'vulnerable set' of LDPC variable nodes with extra perturbation energy reduces required attack power versus untargeted allocation, across all tested modulation/code-rate combinations and both tasks.
core_contribution: The paper challenges the prevailing assumption that semantic communication (DeepJSCC-based) is inherently more vulnerable to adversarial attacks than classical separate source-channel coding (SSCC) systems, because it is built on deep neural networks. Its own contribution is threefold: (1) a theoretical bound, derived via Lipschitz continuity of the DeepJSCC decoder and a first-order Taylor expansion of the attack effect, on the minimum attack power needed to force a target distortion, plus a sufficient condition under which semantic communication provably outperforms classical systems adversarially, tracing the robustness to implicit smoothness regularization induced by noisy-channel training; (2) two new attack methods needed to make the comparison fair and rigorous -- a structure-aware 'vulnerable set' attack that is (per the authors) the first to exploit graph-theoretic weaknesses (short cycles, weak constraint subspaces in the Tanner graph) of LDPC codes to induce decoding failure with minimal energy, and a progressive gradient ascent (PGA) attack that uses DeepJSCC's differentiability to efficiently find minimum-power perturbations (contrasted against a C&W-style baseline attack and, for classical systems, an RL/DQN-based GMS attack); (3) an empirical comparison on two tasks (CIFAR-10 image transmission and massive MIMO CSI feedback) under matched bandwidth/power/channel conditions showing semantic communication requires up to 14-16x more attack power than classical LDPC-coded systems to reach the same target distortion.
threat_model: White-box adversary that has full knowledge of the coding scheme (either the classical SSCC pipeline or the DeepJSCC encoder/decoder weights) and observes the received signal r, then injects an additive perturbation s into the wireless channel (y = r + s) to craft a sample-specific worst-case attack. The adversary's goal is to cause a target reconstruction distortion D* while minimizing its own injected power rho = E[||s||^2]; robustness is measured as the minimum attack power rho* needed to reach D*. This is an attacker-manipulates-the-channel-signal threat model (physical/channel-layer adversarial perturbation), not a training-data-poisoning or model-extraction threat model, and it explicitly does not model imperceptibility constraints (unlike vision adversarial-example work) -- only a power budget.
limitations: Explicitly noted or evident in the paper: (1) the theoretical bound relies on a first-order Taylor/linearization approximation of the DeepJSCC decoder around small perturbations, so it is an approximate, local analysis rather than an exact characterization; (2) no closed-form distortion-vs-attack-power expression exists for classical systems either, so the classical-side comparison is also bound-based rather than exact; (3) the threat model is a channel/signal-injection white-box attacker with only a power budget, not an imperceptibility-constrained adversarial-example setting as in vision, and not a training-time poisoning or extraction threat; (4) evaluation is limited to two tasks (CIFAR-10 image reconstruction and a simulated single-cell massive MIMO CSI feedback scenario using the COST 2100 model), a single decoder architecture family (CNN with residual blocks), and regular LDPC codes only (irregular LDPC/polar/other structured codes are not evaluated, and the authors note vulnerable-set node-degree weighting w1=0 specifically because a regular code has no degree variation to exploit); (5) the paper's own conclusion section frames the robustness gap as a starting point rather than a solved problem, explicitly posing open questions about whether robustness can be deliberately enhanced further (e.g., via smoothness regularization or adversarial training) and whether structured codes can be redesigned to be attack-resilient -- i.e. the authors themselves do not claim the current robustness gap is a mature or final defense.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (60,000 32x32x3 RGB images; 50,000 train / 10,000 test) for the image transmission task

COST 2100 channel model (outdoor rural scenario, 300MHz band) generated CSI matrices for the massive MIMO CSI feedback task, with 100,000 training / 30,000 validation / 20,000 testing samples, Nt=32 antennas, Nc=1024 subcarriers, 32x32 angular-delay representation
quantitative_results: Image task, 12dB SNR: vulnerable-set attack on classical (QPSK, 5/6-LDPC) rho* ~= 7.5e-3 vs. RL-based GMS attack rho* ~= 2.2e-2 (~3x reduction in attack power for vulnerable-set attack)

Image task, 12dB SNR: semantic system (PGA attack) rho*_sem,1 ~= 0.11 vs. classical system (vulnerable-set attack) rho*_sscc,1 ~= 7.5e-3 -- semantic communication is ~14x harder to attack to reach 15dB PSNR target (D*~=3e-2 MSE)
CSI task, 10dB SNR: semantic system rho*_sem,1 ~= 0.5 vs. classical system (vulnerable-set attack) rho*_sscc,1 ~= 3.1e-2 -- semantic communication is >16x harder to attack to reach -16dB NMSE target
CSI task: vulnerable-set attack requires up to 2x less power than the GMS attack across all SNRs (vs. ~3x on the image task)

Ablations (both tasks, all modulation/LDPC-rate combinations: 1/2 16-QAM, 2/3 16-QAM, 5/6 16-QAM, 5/6 QPSK): allocating extra perturbation energy to the precomputed vulnerable-set LDPC variable nodes consistently reduces required attack power vs. an untargeted baseline, with gains more pronounced at higher code rates
baselines_compared: Classical SSCC: BPG source coding + regular LDPC channel coding (rates 1/2, 2/3, 5/6) with QPSK or 16-QAM modulation
RL-based GMS attack (DQN-trained) as the baseline attack on classical systems, compared against the paper's own structure-aware vulnerable-set attack

C&W-style attack (Adam optimizer, adapted from classification adversarial-example literature) as the baseline attack on semantic systems, compared against the paper's own progressive gradient ascent (PGA) attack
